title: TPU v1 · Regression, debug, and bug management: nightly automation, failure triage, tracked closure, and tapeout convergence
oneLine: Verification is not complete after one green run; it is a continuously operating closed loop. Every night, large directed and constrained-random suites exercise TPU's 256×256 array, Unified Buffer, handshakes, and dozen-plus CISC instructions. Failures are triaged into design versus environment/stimulus bugs, assigned by owner and severity, and tracked to closure. Every fix returns to regression. Daily cumulative-found versus cumulative-closed curves must converge before tapeout is credible.

or</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="107">environment/stimulus bug?</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="598" y1="118" x2="598" y2="158" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="db" data-g="db"><rect class="cs-box" x="508" y="162" width="180" height="74" rx="8" fill="#fef2f2" stroke="#fca5a5"/><text class="cs-t" x="598" y="190">Bug database</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="209">Ownership / severity / owner</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="225">State: new→fix→verify→close</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="508" y1="199" x2="444" y2="199" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="fix" data-g="fix"><rect class="cs-box" x="290" y="162" width="150" height="74" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="365" y="190">Diagnose / fix</text><text class="cs-sub" x="365" y="209">Minimal failing waveform</text><text class="cs-sub" x="365" y="225">Change RTL or environment</text></g><polyline class="cs-arr" points="290,199 250,199 250,81 222,81" fill="none" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><text class="cs-note" x="232" y="150">Return fix to regression (loop)</text><g class="cs-part" data-k="curve" data-g="curve"><rect class="cs-box" x="32" y="252" width="656" height="138" rx="8" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#cbd5e1"/><text class="cs-t" x="360" y="276">Bug discovery/closure convergence (illustrative)—tapeout health</text><polyline points="70,300 130,300 190,304 250,312 310,322 370,334 430,346 490,354 550,360 610,364 650,366" fill="none" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/><polyline points="70,372 130,360 190,348 250,338 310,330 370,326 430,324 490,323 550,322 610,322 650,322" fill="none" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/><text class="cs-sub" x="92" y="316">Cumulative found (red, slowing)</text><text class="cs-sub" x="560" y="384">Cumulative closed (green, catching up)</text><text class="cs-note" x="540" y="338">Curves meet → approaching signoff</text></g></svg>

legend.name: Nightly regression (high-volume automation)
legend.desc: Rerun the accumulated suite on the latest RTL every night: constrained-random matrices across precisions/dimensions, full 256 and partial tiles; directed 32-bit accumulator overflow/saturation corners; legal, illegal, and back-to-back CISC sequences; handshakes and CDC. Fixed seeds reproduce every failure exactly. Regression is verification's heartbeat—one green run is insufficient; every night must remain green.

legend.name: Pass / Fail decision (Scoreboard)
legend.desc: A bit-exact Reference Model and Scoreboard automatically judge each DUT result element/transaction, with SVA violations also counted as failures. Thousands of tests produce one report of passes, failures, and newly failing regressions. A test that passed yesterday and fails today often exposes a newly introduced defect.

legend.name: Automated triage (failure classification)
legend.desc: Not every failure is a design defect. Triage classifies RTL behavior errors versus verification-environment defects such as a wrong model, protocol-violating stimulus, Scoreboard error, or missing constraint. Clustering by assertion, signal, and call-stack signature collapses hundreds of symptoms into a few root causes and assigns the right owner.

legend.name: Bug database (tracked to closure)
legend.desc: Each confirmed root cause becomes a tracked record with design/environment ownership, severity, owner, reproducer and seed, and lifecycle: new → diagnosis → fix → verification → closed. It is the single source of truth. A defect outside the database lacks ownership; a record not closed is not fixed.

legend.name: Bug-convergence curves (tapeout criterion)
legend.desc: Plot cumulative discovered and cumulative closed bugs each day. In a healthy project, the discovery slope falls, closure catches discovery, the curves meet, and new-defect rate approaches zero. These curves are the clearest tapeout health indicator; a steep discovery curve means unknown silicon risk remains.

steps.cap: ① Every night, automatically rerun constrained-random matrices + directed corners + instruction/handshake sequences on current RTL. The Scoreboard compares each test with the Reference Model and produces a report including newly failing regressions.
② Automatically triage every failure: cluster duplicate signatures and classify design bugs versus environment/stimulus defects, reducing hundreds of failures to a few root causes.
③ Enter each root cause into the bug database with ownership, severity, owner, and reproduction seed; track new → diagnosis → fix → verification → closed until genuine closure.
④ After diagnosis and correction, replay the minimal failing trace and the full relevant regression. Confirm the fix and absence of new regressions, returning to the Nightly loop.
⑤ Inspect convergence daily: slower discovery, closure catching discovery, curves meeting, and new defects approaching zero are hard evidence that the design is ready for tapeout.

before: Define regression scope and cadence: choose full versus smoke suites, nightly RTL freeze point, compute budget, and reproducible seed policy. Regression must be scheduled, automatic, and traceable—not an occasional manual run—so it provides a stable health signal.

Define failure-triage rules: specify design versus environment classification, signature keys such as assertion/signal/seed, deduplication, and a triage owner for each block. Ambiguous rules turn high-volume failures into unowned repeated work.

Define bug fields and lifecycle: require ownership, severity, owner, reproducer + seed, and linked regression batch. Establish new → diagnosis → fix → verification → closed and the exact evidence required for closure.

Define convergence criteria: quantify closure—for example N consecutive green nights, new-bug rate below threshold, no unresolved high-severity defects, and simultaneous coverage closure. Predefined criteria prevent subjective tapeout decisions.
during: Rule: every random test must be reproducible. Record and fix each Nightly constrained-random seed so every failure replays identically. A one-time disappearing failure cannot be debugged; reproducibility is the foundation of the regression loop.

Rule: triage and deduplicate before filing bugs. Hundreds of failures often represent only a few root causes. Cluster by signature, assign ownership, and file only distinct causes; otherwise owners drown in duplicate records.

Distinguish design bugs from environment bugs carefully. Mislabeling a wrong model, missing constraint, or Scoreboard defect as RTL can corrupt a correct design. Conversely, constraining away a genuine RTL defect hides silicon risk. Incorrect triage can be worse than an uninvestigated failure.

Regress every fix to prevent regression. Rerun the reproducer and the relevant/full suite. The most dangerous report entry is “passed yesterday, fails today,” usually a direct side effect of the current change.

Track the shape of discovery and closure curves daily. Healthy: discovery slope slows, closure catches up, and curves meet. Dangerous: discovery remains steep or closure persistently lags. Curve shape is more informative than today's raw fix count.

Scope note: Nightly regression, automated triage, bug lifecycles, and convergence curves are standard verification-engineering practice for large accelerator ASICs. TPU v1's array, Unified Buffer, handshakes, and CISC interfaces are public verification targets; its regression framework, compute budget, and bug counts are not disclosed.

result.label: Regression model
result.value: Automated Nightly full suite · reproducible fixed seeds · regression report

result.label: Decision method
result.value: Scoreboard checks each test against reference · report includes new failures

result.label: Triage output
result.value: Cluster duplicate signatures → classify design bug / environment bug

result.label: Bug fields (illustrative)
result.value: Ownership · severity · owner · reproduction seed · state (new→fix→verify→close)

result.label: Convergence curve
result.value: Cumulative found vs. cumulative closed meet · new rate approaches zero

result.label: Tapeout gate
result.value: N consecutive green nights · no open critical bugs · coverage closes simultaneously

after: Require stable, all-green regression: run the complete suite on frozen RTL for multiple consecutive nights without new or intermittent failures, proving stable convergence rather than one lucky pass.
Confirm the bug database has no unresolved items: verify every record reached closed with regression evidence. Every high-severity open bug needs an explicit disposition before silicon.
Include convergence curves in signoff evidence: submit the meeting discovery/closure curves and near-zero new rate together with coverage reports as quantitative tapeout evidence.
Combine bug and coverage closure: functional verification passes only when bug curves converge and functional + code coverage targets close. Then the design may proceed to synthesis (⑦).

Preserve regression assets and evidence boundaries: permanently retain every defect-revealing test/seed. Distinguish public TPU structures from standard regression and bug-management methodology rather than presenting the latter as private TPU disclosure.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (TPU v1's systolic array, Unified Buffer, handshakes, and CISC interface are public architectural facts; Nightly regression, failure triage, bug databases, and convergence curves are standard verification practices, while TPU's regression and bug-management details are not public)
